html:
Elevate's Rubber Roofing Roots on Worcester Buildings

Holcim Elevate, formerly known as Firestone Building Products, carries decades of EPDM manufacturing history under the RubberGard name, and that heritage is a real factor on a Worcester reroof list where black EPDM recover jobs over older buildings are still common work for us.

RubberGard EPDM's Long Track Record

Elevate's RubberGard EPDM line has been installed on commercial roofs across New England for decades, and a fair number of the mill-conversion and older institutional buildings we work on around Worcester are already carrying an Elevate or Firestone-era EPDM roof from an earlier reroof cycle. That installed base gives us a longer real-world data set on how the specific membrane performs through repeated New England freeze-thaw seasons than a newer product line would have.

Elevate also manufactures UltraPly TPO, so the company isn't a single-chemistry manufacturer; the EPDM heritage is a strength on recover jobs specifically, not the entirety of what they offer.

TPO and PVC Lines Alongside the Rubber Heritage

UltraPly TPO covers standard and fleece-backed options in the thickness range we spec on most Worcester reroofs, and Elevate's PVC offerings, where applicable to a job's chemical-exposure needs, round out a product line that competes directly with Carlisle, GAF, and Versico on most bids.

We compare Elevate's pricing against those other manufacturers job by job rather than assuming the EPDM heritage translates into a better deal across every membrane type.

Seam Tape Systems for Cold-Weather EPDM Work

Elevate manufactures its own seam tape and primer system for RubberGard EPDM, engineered for the temperature and adhesion requirements EPDM tape seams need. We follow Elevate's published minimum-temperature guidance for that tape system on every cold-weather EPDM install, since a tape seam applied outside the manufacturer's stated window is more likely to fail early through repeated Worcester freeze-thaw cycling.

  • Recover jobs over existing Firestone or Elevate EPDM roofs
  • Owners wanting a manufacturer with a long EPDM installed track record
  • Buildings needing UltraPly TPO for a standard mechanically attached reroof
  • Portfolios standardizing membrane brand across multiple older buildings
  • Roofs where cold-weather tape-seam procedure needs manufacturer-specific guidance

Insulation Products and Code Compliance

Elevate also manufactures polyiso insulation board that pairs with its membrane lines, and we spec board thickness against the applicable energy code, including the higher minimum under the Massachusetts stretch code where Worcester and neighboring towns have adopted it, independent of which manufacturer's membrane sits on top.

Elevate's board pricing runs close to Carlisle's and Johns Manville's on most bids, and we choose between manufacturers based on thickness availability and job-specific lead times rather than a fixed preference for any one brand's insulation line.

Warranty and Certification With Elevate

Warranty length on an Elevate system depends on the specific assembly and our certification status as an installer at the time of install, the same as with any single-ply manufacturer. We confirm certification status directly with Elevate before putting a warranty number in a proposal, since certification tiers can change over time.

We document that certification status in writing as part of every Elevate proposal, since an owner comparing bids across manufacturers deserves to see the same coverage information regardless of which brand a given contractor is pushing.

Fleece-Backed UltraPly and PVC Options for Recover Work

Elevate's UltraPly TPO line includes a fleece-backed option built for recover work over an existing roof, which comes up regularly on Worcester's older commercial buildings where the deck and insulation below an aging membrane are still sound. The fleece layer resists punctures from an uneven or ballasted substrate below, and we spec it specifically where that substrate condition calls for the added protection rather than defaulting to fleece-backed sheet on every recover.

Elevate's recover specifications require the same substrate moisture check we run for any manufacturer's fleece-backed system, since installing over a wet deck voids the practical benefit of recovering rather than tearing off in the first place. Elevate also manufactures a PVC membrane line for roofs with grease, oil, or chemical exposure, rounding out a product catalog that spans all three major single-ply chemistries.

On a hospital or lab roof where chemical resistance matters more than membrane cost, we compare Elevate's PVC pricing against Sarnafil's more PVC-focused line before recommending either one. Elevate's PVC seam welding follows the same manufacturer-published temperature guidance we apply to every thermoplastic membrane, adjusted for Worcester's cold-weather installation windows during shoulder-season and winter jobs.

Questions About Elevate's Product Line

Is Elevate the same company as Firestone Building Products?

Elevate was formerly Firestone Building Products' roofing division; the RubberGard EPDM product line carries that heritage under the Elevate name.

Does Elevate only make EPDM, or other membrane types too?

They also manufacture UltraPly TPO and other membrane and insulation products; EPDM is a historical strength, not their only offering.

Is EPDM from an established manufacturer more reliable than a newer product?

A longer installed track record gives us more real-world performance data in this specific climate, though correct installation matters more to long-term performance than manufacturer history alone.

How does Elevate's insulation help meet Worcester's energy code?

Their polyiso boards come in thicknesses that can meet the stretch code's higher minimum; we calculate the required thickness for each building rather than using a default.

What determines my warranty length on an Elevate system?

The specific assembly spec and our certification status with Elevate at time of install, both of which we verify before finalizing a proposal.
